text: As brilliantly highlighted in many articles, such as that published by Zeegers Paget et al., 1 many important things have been learned nearly 2 years after the emergence of the coronavirus disease (COVID-19) pandemic outbreak, though several aspects remained unrevealed. Several lines of evidence now attest that physical measures (e.g. social distancing, face masking and so forth) are only partially effective for preventing SARS-CoV-2 (severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2) infection and complications, 2 so that widespread COVID-19 vaccination shall be considered the most effective strategy for decreasing virus circulation and preventing or limiting the risk of developing severe COVID-19 illness. 3 Despite the efficacy of COVID-19 vaccines is now straightforward and unquestionable, even higher than that of vaccines used for preventing other respiratory infectious diseases such as Influenza, 4 waning immunity against SARS-CoV-2 has been widely reported, especially 4–6 months after completing the primary COVID-19 vaccination cycle, 5 so that there is ongoing debate as to whether additional vaccine booster doses may be necessary to restore the immune response to levels similar to or even greater than those achievable immediately after completing a primary COVID-19 vaccination. 5 This article is hence aimed at providing an updated analysis of primary COVID-19 vaccination and vaccine booster doses efficacy by analyzing data emerging from the ongoing Italian nationwide COVID-19 vaccination campaign.

metadata.title: Primary COVID-19 vaccine cycle and booster doses efficacy: analysis of Italian nationwide vaccination campaign.

text: We downloaded and analytically reviewed the official statistics of the COVID-19 national integrated surveillance programme, published and regularly updated by the Italian National Institute of Health (Istituto Superiore di Sanità, ISS; Last available update, November 19, 2021), before diffusion of Omicron (B.1.1.529) variant. 6 This bulletin contains comprehensive nationwide epidemiological data related to overall number of SARS-CoV-2 infections, COVID-19-related hospitalizations (not all SARS-CoV-2 infections result in COVID-19-related hospitalizations), intensive care unit (ICU) admissions and deaths, along with information on progression of the COVID-19 vaccination campaign, which started on December 27, 2020, and was originally based on administration of the two mRNA-based COVID-19 vaccines manufactured by Pfizer/BioNTech and Moderna, and the two adenovirus-based COVID-19 vaccines manufactured by AstraZeneca and Johnson & Johnson. From October 2021, a national campaign of booster vaccine doses administration has been also initiated in older (i.e. >60 years old) and fragile people (i.e. immunocompromised, with cancer and/or multiple co-morbidities), extended to the entire national population from December 2021. At the time of this analysis, 45.6 million people have completed the primary COVID-19 vaccination cycle (84.4% of the national population in which COVID-19 vaccination is made available; 71.9% with Pfizer/BioNTech, 13.4% with AstraZeneca, 13.0% with Moderna and 1.7% with Johnson & Johnson, respectively), while 5.2 million people have already received a COVID-19 vaccine booster dose.

metadata.title: Primary COVID-19 vaccine cycle and booster doses efficacy: analysis of Italian nationwide vaccination campaign.

text: The official ISS data were imported into an Excel Worksheet (Microsoft Excel; Microsoft, Redmond, WA, USA) and analyzed with MedCalc (Version 20.015; MedCalc Software Ltd., Ostend, Belgium). Statistical significance was set at P < 0.05. The study was carried out in accordance with Helsinki Declaration, under terms of relevant local legislation. The research was based on public ISS data, 6 so that Ethical Committee approval was unnecessary.

metadata.title: Primary COVID-19 vaccine cycle and booster doses efficacy: analysis of Italian nationwide vaccination campaign.

text: As shown in table 1 (and Supplementary figure S1 ), the cumulative primary COVID-19 vaccine cycle efficacy within 6 months from administration was effective to reduce all SARS-CoV-2 infections by 76%, COVID-19-related hospitalizations by 87%, ICU admissions by 92% and deaths by 84%, respectively. After 6 months from completing the primary COVID-19 vaccine cycle, the protection declined to 59% against the risk of developing SARS-CoV-2 infections, 52% against COVID-19-related hospitalizations and 34% against deaths, but remained considerably high, up to 80%, for preventing COVID-19-related ICU admissions.

metadata.title: Primary COVID-19 vaccine cycle and booster doses efficacy: analysis of Italian nationwide vaccination campaign.

text: The cumulative efficacy of COVID-19 vaccine booster dose is summarized in table 1 (and Supplementary figure S1 ). Compared with COVID-19 vaccine efficacy recorded 6 months after completing the primary vaccination cycle, the administration of COVID-19 vaccine booster doses was effective to reduce SARS-CoV-2 infections by 65%, COVID-19-related hospitalizations by 69% and deaths by 97%. A trend towards reduction of COVID-19-related ICU admissions could also be observed (i.e. −67%), though failing to reach statistical significance. Notably, the efficacy of COVID-19 vaccine booster doses did not significantly differ from that recorded within 6 months after completing the primary COVID-19 vaccination ( table 1 ), though it was further effective for significantly reducing by 39% the risk of developing SARS-CoV-2 infection.

metadata.title: Primary COVID-19 vaccine cycle and booster doses efficacy: analysis of Italian nationwide vaccination campaign.

text: The results of this updated analysis of the ongoing Italian nationwide COVID-19 vaccination campaign before diffusion of Omicron (B.1.1.529) variant are in keeping with those recently emerged from the analogous nationwide vaccination campaign carried out in Israel, 7 , 8 and underlie some essential aspects. After 2 years from initiation of nationwide vaccination, with over 45 million COVID-19 vaccine recipients at present out of a national population of nearly 59.2 inhabitants, the primary vaccine efficacy against the risk of developing severe COVID-19 illness (i.e. COVID-19-related hospitalization, ICU admission or death) in Italy is confirmed to be over 84%, and is also associated with a remarkable 76% reduced risk of developing SARS-CoV-2 infection. Despite primary vaccine efficacy at 6 months after completing the primary vaccination cycle remains sizeable, a substantial decline could be observed when all endpoints were compared with those observed within 6 months after primary vaccination ( table 1 ). Such reduction seems however completely reversible by administering COVID-19 vaccine booster doses, which are seemingly effective to generate a cumulative protection very similar (or even better) than that achievable immediately after completing the primary COVID-19 vaccination.

metadata.title: Primary COVID-19 vaccine cycle and booster doses efficacy: analysis of Italian nationwide vaccination campaign.

text: Notably, the administration of additional COVID-19 vaccine booster doses seems also effective to substantially diminish the risk of developing SARS-CoV-2 infection compared with immediate (i.e. within 6 months) primary vaccination efficacy, with such efficacy further increasing to 65% in people who had received a primary COVID-19 vaccination over 6 months before. This aspect is of paramount importance, since reducing the circulation of the virus in the community not only remains primary target of SARS-CoV-2 eradication campaigns, but may also generate additional advantages such as reducing the likelihood of emergence of new variants and the risk of mandating social limitations such as lockdowns or partial closures. 9 Finally, adjunctive vaccine doses will probably become almost unavoidable for restoring COVID-19 vaccine efficacy against emerging and highly mutated SARS-CoV-2 variants, such as Omicron (B.1.1.259), as recently demonstrated in recently published clinical trials. 10 , 11

metadata.title: Primary COVID-19 vaccine cycle and booster doses efficacy: analysis of Italian nationwide vaccination campaign.

text: The COVID-19 pandemic represents a severe global public health challenge. Vaccination is a cornerstone of control, yet the efficacy of primary regimens wanes over time, exacerbated by immune-evasive variants like Omicron ( Andrews et al., 2022 ; Collie et al., 2022 ; Nordström et al., 2022 ). While previous study has showed that heterologous prime-boost strategies with different COVID-19 vaccines enhanced neutralizing antibodies and broaden cross-reactivity ( Jin, Guo, et al., 2023 ; Jin, Tang, et al., 2023 ; Li, Hou, et al., 2022 ; Li, Wu et al., 2022 ) . Although an increasing immune response associated with heterologous prime-boost regimen has been demonstrated, their protective superiority is inherently hampered by the rapid evolution and immune escape of SARS-CoV-2 variants. Conventional clinical trials often lag behind viral evolution, failing to provide timely efficacy data against circulating strains ( Carabelli et al., 2023 ). In addition, efficacy trials of vaccines are costly, time-consuming, and ethical restrictions ( Black, 2015 ). In the context of rapidly evolving viruses and emerging variants, relying solely on clinical trial study is insufficient for timely response to a changing pandemic landscape.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: This critical lag necessitates alternative approaches. Neutralizing antibodies, strongly correlated with protection against infection across vaccine platforms and endorsed as correlates of protection (CoPs) by regulatory agencies, offer a key immunological metric ( Earle et al., 2021 ; Gilbert et al., 2022 ; Regev-Yochay et al., 2023 ). Evidence from natural infection cohorts has demonstrated a strong positive correlation between neutralizing antibody titers and protection against reinfection ( Addetia et al., 2020 ), with high antibody levels effectively preventing reinfection by circulating variants of concern ( Garcia-Beltran et al., 2021 ). Consequently, neutralizing antibodies have been endorsed by both the U.S. Food and Drug Administration and the European Medicines Agency as immunological correlates of protection in support of emergency use authorization for COVID-19 vaccines ( EMA, 2021 ; FDA, 2022 ). Khoury et al. integrated neutralizing antibody data from COVID-19 vaccines to reveal a nonlinear relationship between neutralizing antibodies and vaccine efficacy. The model relies on neutralizing antibody levels against specific strains post-vaccination. When new variants dominated, neutralizing antibody data for the new strain was required to predict efficacy ( Khoury et al., 2021 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: Genetic distance quantifies the sequence divergence between viral variants, reflecting key antigenic site mutations and immune escape potential, and serves as a critical metric for evaluating vaccine-induced immunity ( Cerqueira-Silva, de Araujo Oliveira, et al., 2022 ; Cerqueira-Silva, de Araujo Oliveira, et al., 2022 ; Ella et al., 2021 ; Suphanchaimat et al., 2022 ). Therefore, the “genetic distance between viral variants and quantifying antigenic site divergence and immune escape potential” is a principal determinant of vaccine effectiveness, linking viral evolution to immune protection.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: In this study, we developed a predictive model linking neutralizing antibody levels, genetic distance, and vaccine protection in order to address the challenge of timely efficacy assessment amidst evolving variants. Utilizing neutralization data from previous heterologous booster trials and introducing a refined genetic distance calculation focused on key RBD residues, our model enables rapid evaluation of heterologous booster efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: Neutralizing antibody data from three previously reported heterologous prime-boost clinical trials of COVID-19 vaccines ( ClinicalTrials.gov registration numbers: NCT04892459 ( Li, Wu, et al., 2022 ), NCT04952727 ( Jin, Tang, et al., 2023 ), and NCT05043259 ( Li, Wu, et al., 2022 ); Supplementary Appendix 1 ) were used to construct the exponential decay models of antibody kinetics, which were subsequently applied to vaccine efficacy prediction. The three trial protocols were approved by Research Ethics Committee of Jiangsu Provincial Center for Disease Control and Prevention (JSJK2021-A007-01, JSJK2021-A008-01 and JSJK2021-A024-02), and were conducted following the principles of the Declaration of Helsinki, ICH Good Clinical Practice guidelines and local guidelines. Written informed consent was obtained from all participants. We established a “neutralizing antibody-vaccine efficacy” (NAb-VE) model based on two components: (1) published vaccine efficacy or effectiveness data from COVID-19 priming regimens and heterologous prime-boost regimens; and (2) published live-virus neutralization titres from the corresponding regimens. This model enabled prediction of both the magnitude and durability of protection conferred by different prime-boost vaccination strategies against SARS-CoV-2 variants (i.e. wild-type SARS-CoV-2, Delta, Omicron BA.1) for which neutralizing antibody titres were available.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: Then, in order to extend the model predictions to other variants for which neutralizing antibody titres were unavailable, we established a second efficacy prediction model—the “modified genetic distance-vaccine efficacy” (GD-VE) model based on a combination of three components: (1) published vaccine efficacy or effectiveness data from heterologous COVID-19 prime-boost regimens; (2) predicted efficacy estimates derived from the NAb-VE model; and (3) viral genomic sequence data from the Global Initiative on Sharing All Influenza Data (GISAID) database.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: At last, we developed a sieve analysis framework-the “neutralizing antibody-genetic distance-vaccine efficacy” (NAb-GD-VE) model to predict average vaccine efficacy against symptomatic COVID-19 diseases by integrating the NAb-VE and GD-VE models. Using monthly whole-genome sequencing data during the pandemic and post-pandemic periods (July 2021-July 2023) in Jiangsu Province, China, we estimated temporal changes in efficacy across different vaccination strategies as SARS-CoV-2 variants circulated. This prediction assumed that, for months without reported COVID-19 cases, the genetic distance was taken as identical to that of the preceding month with circulating variants to ensure continuity in monthly estimates. The study evaluated three vaccination regimens: (1) two doses of inactivated CoronaVac followed by an aerosolized Ad5-nCoV booster; (2) two doses of CoronaVac followed by an intramuscular Ad5-nCoV booster; and (3) three doses of homologous CoronaVac.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: In order to establish NAb-VE model and GD-VE model, we systematically searched PubMed and Web of Science for studies on COVID-19 vaccine immunogenicity and efficacy or effectiveness ( Supplementary Appendix 2 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: A total of 23 studies were included to establish the models ( Fig. S3 ). The NAb-VE model was fitted using data from 9 studies reporting live-virus neutralizing antibody titres standardized to convalescent sera (available either directly or indirectly) induced by COVID-19 vaccines ( Angkasekwinai et al., 2024 ; Folegatti et al., 2020 ; Jackson et al., 2020 ; Keech et al., 2020 ; Logunov et al., 2020 ; Sadoff et al., 2021 ; Walsh et al., 2020 ; Wheatley et al., 2021 ; Zhang et al., 2021 ), as well as 9 studies reporting vaccine efficacy or effectiveness against symptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infection ( Baden et al., 2021 ; Jara et al., 2022 ; Logunov et al., 2021 ; Lumley et al., 2021 ; Novavax, 2021 ; Pearson, Magalhaes, & Deng, 2021 ; Polack et al., 2020 ; Sacks, 2021 ; Voysey et al., 2021 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: For the GD-VE model, to enable subsequent application in predicting the efficacy of heterologous boosters after inactivated vaccine priming, we combined two data sources: (i) efficacy estimates derived from the NAb-VE model for three regimens—two doses of CoronaVac followed by aerosolized Ad5-nCoV booster, two doses of CoronaVac followed by intramuscular Ad5-nCoV booster, and three doses of homologous CoronaVac; and (ii) observed effectiveness data from five studies of heterologous prime-boost regimens: two doses of CoronaVac followed by BNT162b2 (reported in all five studies ( Cerqueira-Silva, de Araujo Oliveira, et al., 2022 ; Cerqueira-Silva, de Araujo Oliveira, et al., 2022 ; Jara et al., 2022 ; Sritipsukho et al., 2022 ; Suphanchaimat et al., 2022 )) and two doses of CoronaVac followed by ChAdOx1 (reported in three studies ( Jara et al., 2022 ; Sritipsukho et al., 2022 ; Suphanchaimat et al., 2022 )). A total of 87,036 full-length SARS-CoV-2 genome sequences (FASTA format) corresponding to the time and location of the five studies were downloaded from the GISAID EpiCoV database ( https://gisaid.org/ ) and were used to compute genetic distances for the GD-VE model construction ( Supplementary Appendix 3 ). In addition, 1701 full-length genome sequences were collected from Jiangsu Province, China, between July 2021 and July 2023, were downloaded from the GISAID EpiCoV database. These sequences were used to calculate genetic distances and to apply the NAb-GD-VE sieve model for predicting the dynamic vaccine efficacy during periods of overlapping variant circulation.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: Following the Prentice Criteria outlined in the WHO report on evaluating immunological surrogate endpoints for vaccine protection ( Supplementary Appendix 4 )( WHO, 2013 ), we assessed whether neutralizing antibody titres against live SARS-CoV-2 virus could serve as an immunological surrogate endpoint for protection conferred by COVID-19 vaccination regimens.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: The model used a logistic relationship to link neutralizing antibody levels to protection probability and applied maximum likelihood estimation to fit the model and determine optimal parameters for population-level vaccine efficacy. This model was based on the fundamental assumptions: (i) the vaccine efficacy was primarily mediated by neutralizing antibodies; (ii) live-virus neutralizing antibodies declined exponentially over time; and (iii) their levels were linked to efficacy through a logistic function ( Supplementary Appendix 5 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: The model was internally validated using leave-one-out cross-validation and externally validated with observed vaccine efficacy data from heterologous prime-boost regimens not included in the model. Sensitivity analysis was conducted using the 95% CI bounds of the mean neutralizing antibody levels to predict efficacy ( Supplementary Appendix 5.4 ). Agreement between model-predicted and observed efficacy values was assessed using the Concordance Correlation Coefficient (CCC). This modeling framework was originally proposed by Khoury et al. ( Khoury et al., 2021 ), and we further refined the model by incorporating heterologous prime-boost vaccination data.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: We utilized live virus neutralizing antibody data from three previously conducted clinical trials on heterologous prime-boost COVID-19 vaccination regimens ( ClinicalTrials.gov registration numbers: NCT04892459 ( Li, Hou, et al., 2022 ), NCT04952727 ( Jin, Guo, et al., 2023 ), and NCT05043259 ( Li, Wu, et al., 2022 )) to fit an exponential decay model ( Supplementary Appendix 5.5 ). This was then incorporated into the NAb-VE model to estimate the durability of vaccine-induced protection.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: The genetic distance-based prediction model was constructed using a two-level linear mixed-effects model ( Cao et al., 2022 ). This model relies on three assumptions: (i) immune escape of SARS-CoV-2 variants after heterologous prime-boost regimens was primarily determined by genetic distance; (ii) vaccine efficacy declines linearly with increasing genetic distance; (iii) and covariates such as age and time since booster acted independently of genetic distance ( Supplementary Appendix 6 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: Previous studies have commonly used the Hamming distance to calculate genetic distance between viral sequences, assigning a uniform weight of 1.0 to each amino acid mismatch and computing the average number of mismatches ( Cao et al., 2022 ). In the present study, we accounted for the fact that key mutations tend to accumulate at antigenic sites within the receptor-binding domain (RBD) of SARS-CoV-2 variants—mutations that substantially alter antigenicity and facilitate escape from neutralizing antibodies (NAbs) elicited by repeated vaccination or infection. A total of 13 critical amino acid positions were selected as follows: R346, K378, K417, K444, V445, G446, N450, L452, V483, E484, F486, F490, and Q493 ( Jian et al., 2025 ). These 13 sites repeatedly mutate across multiple SARS-CoV-2 variant lineages, enhancing viral infectivity and increasing escape from monoclonal antibody capture ( Jian et al., 2025 ). To reflect the increased immunological relevance of these sites, we assigned greater weights to mismatches occurring at these critical antigenic positions when calculating the average number of amino acid mismatches, thereby defining a modified genetic distance. Based on the deep mutational scanning (DMS) scores of these key mutations reflecting immune escape in vitro, which differed by up to twofold, we experimentally set the weight coefficients for key antigenic sites to 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0, as no related model parameters have been reported previously ( Dadonaite et al., 2023 ; Jian et al., 2025 ). Using these weights, we recalculated the modified genetic distances and subsequently fitted each to the GD-VE model ( Supplementary Appendix 6.2 ). The performance of each model was evaluated by calculating the CCC between the predicted and validated vaccine efficacy values. The weight yielding the highest CCC was selected as optimal.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: The GD-VE model was validated similarly to the NAb-VE model, using leave-one-out cross-validation, external data, sensitivity analysis, and agreement assessed with the CCC ( Supplementary Appendix 6.4 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: We developed a composite sieve analysis framework of the NAb-GD-VE model using a weighted averaging approach under conditions of overlapping and sequential circulation of diverse SARS-CoV-2 variants ( Supplementary Appendix 7 ). As this model represents an integration of the validated NAb-VE and GD-VE models, no separate validation was performed.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: Molecular phylogenetic analysis of full-length SARS-CoV-2 genomes was performed to explore the relationship between predicted vaccine efficacy and genetic divergence of variants ( Supplementary Appendix 8 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: All statistical analyses and data visualizations were performed using R software (version 4.2.3). When the predicted efficacy boundary exceeds the predefined bounds of 0.00% and 100.00%, a truncation method has been applied to ensure statistical validity. The primary R packages used were listed in Table S4 . The cloud-based online prediction system was developed using Python (version 3.0) with the Flask framework (see Supplementary Appendix 10 for system development details).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: The results showed that neutralizing antibodies can thus serve as valid immunological surrogate endpoints for COVID-19 vaccine protection satisfying the Prentice criteria ( Supplementary Appendix 11 ). In this study, the fitted parameter values for the NAb-VE model were σ = 0.4550, k = 2.2924 (95% CI: 1.8612-2.8234), and n 50 = 0.1784 (95% CI: 0.1192-0.2669) ( Fig. 1 a).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: As the fold decrease in neutralizing antibodies against wild-type SARS-CoV-2 increases, predicted efficacy against symptomatic infection declined for all three regimens: two-dose inactivated CoronaVac plus an aerosolized Ad5-nCoV, two-dose CoronaVac plus one-dose intramuscular Ad5-nCoV, and three-dose CoronaVac, with a more gradual decline for those showing higher protection against the wild-type strain ( Fig. 1 b).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: The internal and external validation results showed close agreement between predicted and observed efficacy ( Fig. 1 c and d). The error was almost always within 5% ( Table S8 and Table S9 ). The calibration plot showed strong concordance between predicted and observed efficacy, with a CCC of 0.95 (95% CI: 0.82-0.98) ( Fig. 1 e). Sensitivity analysis confirmed the robustness of the model predictions ( Fig. 1 f and Table S10 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: The GD-VE model results showed that genetic distances in various SARS-CoV-2 genomic regions, including RBD, were significantly linked to protection efficacy against symptomatic COVID-19 (P < 0.05). RBD genetic distance, influencing viral binding to ACE2, was selected, with each RBD mutation associated with a 2.27% reduction in protection ( Table S11 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: To capture the immunological significance of RBD antigenic sites, we applied weighted schemes to key positions and recalculated genetic distances for GD-VE model fitting. A weight coefficient of 2.0 yielded the highest CCC in validation and was adopted as the optimal weighting strategy ( Table S12 ). With the wild-type SARS-CoV-2 strain as the reference, modified RBD genetic distances for variants ranged from 3 for Delta to 37 for XDV.1 ( Table S13 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: The GD-VE model achieved an R 2 of 85.53% ( Table S15 ) with intercept and error term standard deviations of 28.26 and 13.33, respectively ( Table S17 ). The model's predicted relationship was shown in Fig. 2 a. Regression diagnostics indicated a good fit of the GD-VE model ( Figure S6- S8 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: Internal and external validation showed that across all heterologous prime-boost regimens, predicted and observed efficacy differed by <10%, with overlapping 95% CIs ( Fig. 2 b and Table S20 ; Fig. 2 c and Table S23 ). The CCC between the predicted and observed efficacy values in internal and external validation was 0.93 (95% CI: 0.49-0.99), indicating strong agreement between predicted and actual outcomes ( Fig. 2 d). Sensitivity analyses further confirmed the robustness of the GD-VE model ( Fig. 2 e and Table S24 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: The validated NAb-VE and GD-VE models predicted that, among the three heterologous prime-boost regimens, two doses of CoronaVac followed by an aerosolized Ad5-nCoV booster conferred the highest predictive efficacy against symptomatic COVID-19, followed by the intramuscular Ad5-nCoV booster, whereas the homologous three-dose CoronaVac regimen provided the lowest predictive protection. For the regimen of two doses of CoronaVac followed by an aerosolized Ad5-nCoV, predicted efficacy was 95.40% (95% CI: 92.67%-98.13%) against the wild-type strain, 79.56% (95% CI: 53.31%-100.00%) against Delta, and 68.21% (95% CI: 42.53%-93.89%) against Omicron BA.5.2.20. Against other Omicron sublineages (BA.2.75, BA.1, BF.5 and BF.7), predicted efficacy fell below 50%, and for EG.1.2, XBB.1.16, EG.5.1, JN.1, XDV.1, KP.2, and KP.3, the model predicted little to no protection.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: Predicted efficacy was 87.32% (95% CI: 83.86-90.79) for two doses of CoronaVac followed by an intramuscular Ad5-nCoV booster and 61.40% (56.64-66.17) for three doses of CoronaVac against the wild-type strain. However, for nearly all other variants, both regimens exhibited <50% predicted efficacy or no measurable protection estimates ( Fig. 3 a and Table S25 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: A phylogenetic tree based on whole-genome sequences of 14 SARS-CoV-2 strains and 29,743 nucleotide positions showed that closer evolutionary relationships corresponded to more similar predicted protective efficacy against symptomatic infection under heterologous prime-boost regimens ( Fig. S9 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: Between July 2021 and July 2023 in Jiangsu, China, multiple SARS-CoV-2 variants cross-circulated. In this context, sieve model analyses showed that two doses of CoronaVac followed by an aerosolized Ad5-nCoV booster consistently provided the highest predicted efficacy against symptomatic COVID-19. However, after Omicron sublineages emerged, the predicted efficacy of all three regimens dropped below 50% ( Fig. 4 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: Neutralizing antibody data obtained from clinical trials were used to fit exponential decay models for each SARS-CoV-2 strain, with estimated half-lives summarized in Table S26-S28 .

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: The GD-VE model predicted that two doses of CoronaVac followed by an aerosolized Ad5-nCoV booster provided the most durable estimated protection against symptomatic infection with the wild-type strain, with efficacy of 76.98% (95% CI: 70.32%-83.63%) at 12 months and 36.76% (95% CI: 31.71%-41.81%) at 24 months post-boost. Efficacy at 12 months for the two doses of CoronaVac followed by an intramuscular Ad5-nCoV and three-dose CoronaVac regimens was 24.39% (95% CI: 17.85%-30.94%) and 9.33% (95% CI: 3.34%-15.33%), respectively, both declining to nearly zero by 24 months post-boost ( Fig. 3 c and Table S29 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: For the Delta variant, two doses of CoronaVac followed by an intramuscular Ad5-nCoV regimen yielded predicted efficacies below 50% throughout, while the three-dose CoronaVac regimen remained consistently below 15% ( Fig. 3 d and Table S30 ). Durability efficacy estimates for the two doses of CoronaVac followed by an aerosolized Ad5-nCoV regimen were not analyzed for Delta, as neutralizing antibody measurements for this variant were not pre-specified in the protocol.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: For the Omicron BA.1 variant, both the two doses of CoronaVac followed by an aerosolized or intramuscular Ad5-nCoV regimens conferred relatively low levels of predicted protection, while the homologous three-dose CoronaVac regimen provided virtually none ( Fig. 3 e and Table S31 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: We further assumed that the half-life of neutralizing antibodies against variants with different genetic distances was the same as that against the wild-type strain, enabling prediction of vaccine efficacy over time and genetic distance. Predicted efficacy declined with both time and increasing genetic distance across all three regimens ( Fig. 3 b).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: Additionally, we developed a cloud-based online prediction platform for the NAb-VE model, accessible at: http://8.153.70.192:1003/ . The platform requires no login, and international users can directly perform efficacy predictions. The system functionality and interface were shown in Fig. S10 . We demonstrated the utility of the platform using two case examples, which confirmed its reliable predictive performance ( Supplementary Appendix 20 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: The ongoing evolution of SARS-CoV-2 and the risk of future respiratory pandemics highlight the need for efficient tools to evaluate vaccine-induced protection ( Bardhan et al., 2024 ; WHO, 2024 ). This study established a predictive framework that integrates neutralizing antibody titers and viral genetic distance to estimate the efficacy and durability of heterologous prime-boost immunization. This model enables real-time estimation of protection against symptomatic infection caused by specific SARS-CoV-2 variants, quantifies the durability of efficacy across heterologous prime-boost regimens, and evaluates average efficacy under overlapping variant circulation. The framework provides a scalable approach to inform vaccine deployment in future outbreaks.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: In developing the neutralizing antibody-based protection model, we built upon the framework established by Khoury et al. ( Khoury et al., 2021 ), optimizing model parameters using data from heterologous prime-boost immunization studies and adjusting for covariates such as pre-existing Ad5 immunity, which may impair the generation of protective antibodies induced by Ad5-nCoV( Li et al., 2023 ; Tang et al., 2023 ). Our optimized model better reflects the characteristics of heterologous boosting with adenoviral vector following inactivated vaccine priming.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: In this study, we developed a genetic distance-based protection prediction model to estimate the efficacy of different heterologous prime-boost regimens against emerging variants with unknown neutralizing antibody levels, thereby addressing a limitation of the neutralization-based models. We found that amino acid substitutions in the RBD significantly affected the predicted efficacy of heterologous regimen against symptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infection—consistent with previous findings in the context of primary vaccination ( Cao et al., 2022 ). The RBD is the principal target of neutralizing antibodies, and most COVID-19 vaccines (e.g., mRNA and inactivated vaccines) confer protection primarily by inducing RBD-specific neutralizing antibodies ( Liang et al., 2025 ; Liu et al., 2023 ). Mutations in the RBD can alter antibody-binding sites, reducing the binding affinity of neutralizing antibodies and thereby diminishing vaccine-induced protection ( Ao et al., 2022 ). SARS-CoV-2 variants continue to accumulate recurrent mutations at key antigenic sites within the RBD, leading to increased infectivity and enhanced escape from both monoclonal antibodies and vaccine or infection-induced neutralizing antibodies ( Jian et al., 2023 , 2025 ; Kaku et al., 2023 ; Yang et al., 2024 ). Our framework is the first to define a modified RBD genetic distance that assigns greater weight to key antigenic sites, enhancing the metric's ability to capture biologically meaningful variation relevant to immune escape and vaccine performance.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: This study establishes a cloud-based prediction system for rapid estimation of vaccine efficacy (with 95% CIs) against specific SARS-CoV-2 variants via an intuitive web interface. The platform delivers real-time visualization, stable performance, and requires no statistical expertise. Its generalizable framework allows adaptation to other respiratory pathogens following parameter validation, serving as a scalable solution for vaccine evaluation and pandemic preparedness. By leveraging existing viral surveillance infrastructure, this system enables real-time efficacy assessment during multi-variant circulation and provides a technical foundation for evaluating vaccines against future emerging respiratory threats.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: This study has several limitations. Firstly, WHO reference standards may cause slight underestimation of predicted efficacy, though this effect attenuates at higher antibody levels. Secondly, the neutralization models used clinical trial efficacy data while genetic distance models incorporated real-world effectiveness data. Thirdly, this study is limited by its focus on heterologous sequential booster regimens following inactivated COVID-19 vaccines, primarily due to data availability. Expanding the external validation dataset to include additional vaccine types and diverse population characteristics would be essential for improving the model's generalizability and prediction accuracy in various scenarios. This limitation should be addressed in future research. Finally, a significant limitation of our model is the exclusive focus on humoral immunity, which does not account for the roles of cellular immunity (e.g., T-cell responses) and mucosal immunity. Both cellular and mucosal immunity play crucial roles in the protection against SARS-CoV-2 infection, particularly in preventing severe disease and influencing viral clearance. Excluding these immune responses could lead to an incomplete understanding of vaccine efficacy, especially against variants with immune escape capabilities. Future models should integrate data on T-cell responses, mucosal immunity, and their interactions with neutralizing antibodies to provide a more comprehensive evaluation of vaccine-induced protection. This would help reduce potential biases and enhance the accuracy and applicability of the prediction.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: This study developed a framework for evaluating the efficacy of heterologous prime-boost COVID-19 vaccination based on neutralizing antibodies and viral genetic distance. By leveraging existing viral surveillance networks, the framework enables real-time estimation of vaccine efficacy under co-circulating variant scenarios and offers a technical foundation for timely evaluation of vaccine efficacy against future emerging respiratory pathogens.

text: Medical errors (MEs) have been reported as the third leading cause of death in the United States [ 1 , 2 ]. These MEs can result in psychological and physical harm to patients as well as severe financial loss. About one in 10 patients experiences harm in a primary or ambulatory healthcare setting, and over three million deaths occur annually worldwide due to inadequate provisions for patient safety [ 3 ]. The majority (> 50%) of these harmful medical events are preventable, and many are attributed to diagnostic or medication errors, unsafe surgical procedures, care-related infections, patient falls, pressure ulcers, patient misidentification, unsafe blood transfusion, and venous thromboembolism [ 3 ]. Globally, MEs may account for trillions of US dollars in costs annually, underscoring the urgent need to make patient safety a priority across all healthcare settings [ 3 ].

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Sentinel events (SEs), the most severe form of MEs, are safety events unrelated to the patient’s underlying condition that may result in death or serious harm, including permanent or substantial temporary injury [ 1 ]. Examples of SEs include wrong surgery, involving the wrong site, procedure, patient, or implant, unintended retention of foreign objects, and delay in treatment [ 4 ]. In 2023, the Joint Commission on Accreditation of Health Care Organizations (JCHAO) received 1,411 SE reports [ 4 ]. Of all SEs reported, 88% occurred in hospitals, and most were reported voluntarily [ 4 ]. The leading SE was patient falls (48%), followed by surgical error and unintended retention of objects (8% each). Surgical errors increased by 26% and foreign objects by 11% from 2022 [ 4 ]. Other frequent SEs included treatment delays (6%), fire or burn from medical equipment (4%), and errors in medication management (2%) [ 4 ].

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Patient safety is a priority across all healthcare settings, encompassing the prevention of harm, reduction of errors, and assurance of high-quality care. Protecting patients from avoidable complications, ensuring effective communication, reducing pain, suffering, and risk of harm during routine and complex care, and minimizing possible financial repercussions remain a focus of all healthcare professionals (HCPs). One strategy for promoting patient safety is through patient advocacy (PA). PA is an ethical, professional responsibility in which HCPs act to safeguard patients’ rights, promote their interests, and support their access to appropriate care, especially in situations where patients may be vulnerable, underserved, or unable to speak for themselves [ 5 ]. PA also encompasses the preservation of autonomy, empowering patients to be involved in decisions about their care [ 6 - 8 ].

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Optimizing safety protects patients from preventable harm and positively impacts health outcomes and trust with HCPs [ 3 , 8 , 9 ]. Promoting safety requires a structured framework of organized activities that foster specific behaviors, culture, and environment. This framework encompasses procedures and technologies that minimize risk, prevent avoidable harm to patients, and decrease the occurrence of medical errors [ 3 ]. Currently available literature describes the efficacy of communication strategies [ 10 , 11 ], but provides a limited understanding of the relationship between HCPs’ specific PA practices and their impact on patient safety.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Promoting PA as a culture may positively impact safety and help prevent avoidable SEs and MEs within organizations [ 1 , 12 ]. However, time limitations and job pressures may influence HCPs’ ability to provide effective advocacy [ 13 ]. As a result, patients may receive lower quality care resulting in poorer outcomes [ 12 , 13 ]. HCPs must advocate for their patients to promote high-quality care and minimize preventable errors. This systematic review aims to synthesize existing evidence on current PA practices to inform strategies for improving patient safety. Despite growing recognition of PA as a critical determinant of safety, how advocacy practices are enacted across HCP roles and clinical settings remains poorly defined. Existing research often explores advocacy conceptually or within specific disciplines, but it rarely examines the specific practices through which HCPs advocate for patient safety across diverse clinical settings and professional roles. Current literature lacks an integrated synthesis across HCP roles and settings, leading to a fragmented understanding of how advocacy practices can be operationalized and sustained at a systems-level. By addressing this gap and examining the literature for patterns of advocacy behaviors that demonstrably promote patient safety, this systematic review and thematic synthesis seek to identify evidence-based strategies that enable HCPs to enhance the effectiveness of their advocacy practices to facilitate patient safety.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: This systematic review and thematic synthesis evaluated peer-reviewed published literature that included PA practices of HCPs in different healthcare settings worldwide. The review followed guidelines from the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) [ 14 ], and the PRISMA in Exercise, Rehabilitation, Sport medicine and SporTs science (PERSiST) [ 15 ]. The review was registered on PROSPERO (CRD42024618794).

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Eligible studies investigated the attributes, perceptions, and behaviors of HCPs who advocated for patient safety across qualitative and cross-sectional study designs. Studies evaluated physicians and/or nurses of any specialty and healthcare setting. No age restrictions were applied to study participants; some included studies involved patient participants of varying ages. Studies identified PA practices that facilitated patient safety in hospitals, nursing homes, or long-term care clinics. Studies were excluded if the participant characteristics specifically involved other health professions such as pharmacists, radiologists, dentists, or technicians. Interventions using artificial intelligence (AI) or simulation, mobile apps, telehealth, or training programs were also excluded.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: A university librarian assisted in developing search terms as seen in Table 1 . CINAHL Complete, and EBSCO MegaFILE databases were searched on 26 September 2025, and a 'peer-reviewed' filter was applied. The PubMed database search was performed on 13 March 2026 using the librarian-developed search string with no filters applied. No limits were placed on publication year, language, or study type.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: At least two reviewers independently screened 937 articles identified in the searches. Duplicates and conference abstracts were excluded prior to screening. Titles and abstracts were reviewed for relevance and excluded if the following criteria were met: irrelevant topic, presentation abstract, editorial, or no English translation. Two additional records were identified through supplementary searching conducted prior to the formal librarian-assisted database search. These records were assessed using the same predefined eligibility criteria applied to all database-retrieved records. Duplicate checking was conducted against the full set of database-identified records using title, author, and DOI verification. Both studies were confirmed as peer-reviewed publications and met all inclusion criteria prior to inclusion.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Data extraction from eligible studies was supported by structured automation tools to improve efficiency and reduce transcription errors, while interpretive judgment and final data decisions were made by the first author. Data extraction for studies identified in the original search was assisted using Afforai AI Research Assistant and Elicit [ 16 , 17 ]. Data extraction for studies identified in the updated search was supported using Logically (formerly Afforai) and Elicit [ 17 , 18 ]. Data extraction for the additionally identified study was supported using Logically [ 18 ]. Across all search phases, these automation tools were used solely to facilitate information retrieval and organization; the authors retained full responsibility for data verification, inclusion decision, and interpretation. Extracted variables included author, year, study design, participants (sample size, age, sex), setting, and qualitative outcome.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Risk of bias was assessed with Critical Appraisal Skills Programme (CASP) checklist for Qualitative Research [ 19 ], and CASP checklist for Descriptive/Cross-Sectional Studies [ 20 ].

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: The primary qualitative outcome was the identification of advocacy practices of HCPs to enhance patient safety. Key themes across studies were synthesized using affinity mapping, in which each relevant finding was grouped according to thematic relatedness. ChatGPT (GPT-5, OpenAI, 2025) was used to assist with consolidating and drafting narrative summaries of author-derived themes following thematic grouping. The first author independently identified subthemes pertaining to the research question across all included studies, organized them into five major thematic groupings, and color-coded subthemes by thematic similarity prior to AI-assisted consolidation. ChatGPT was used to generalize the grouped subthemes into consolidated summary statements, which the first author cross-checked against the original groupings to ensure accurate representation. For the additionally identified study, subthemes were independently identified and grouped according to the existing major themes, and integrated into the synthesis with support from Claude (Anthropic, 2026), with adjustments made to affected thematic items as needed. All thematic synthesis, interpretation, and final analytic decisions were conducted by the authors.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Confidence in qualitative and cross-sectional findings was assessed using the Confidence in the Evidence from Reviews of Qualitative Research (GRADE-CERQual), considering methodological limitations, coherence, adequacy of data, and relevance [ 21 ]. Domains were graded according to the level of concern about their potential impact on confidence in review findings.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Database searches identified 937 articles, 540 of which were screened after removing 397 duplicates, conference abstracts, and poster presentations. Per eligibility criteria, 517 articles were excluded following review of titles and abstracts. Full text was assessed in 23 articles, 14 of which met eligibility criteria, while nine did not address the research question. Along with two records identified through supplementary searching, the review included 16 studies (Figure 1 ).

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: The final analysis included 14 qualitative research studies [ 22 - 35 ] and two cross-sectional studies [ 36 , 37 ]. Sample size ranged from six to 244 participants. All studies involved nurses; four studies also included physicians. The studies were conducted in 13 countries including the United States [ 22 ], Sweden [ 23 , 32 ], Japan [ 24 ], Australia [ 25 ], Ghana [ 26 ], United Kingdom [ 27 ], Saudi Arabia [ 28 ], South Africa [ 29 ], Iran [ 30 , 33 ], China [ 31 , 37 ], Italy [ 34 ], the Netherlands [ 35 ], and Nigeria [ 36 ].

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Qualitative studies used semi-structured interviews, naïve sketches, focus groups, non-participant observations, and shadowed observations with contextual inquiries [ 22 - 35 ]. Cross-sectional studies used self-reported questionnaires with or without narrative responses, and examined HCPs’ knowledge, practice of advocacy roles, and self-assessed performance across CARE model dimensions (competence, altruism, responsibility, empathy) [ 36 , 37 ]. Settings across all studies included inpatient hospitals (tertiary, acute, pediatric, university), surgical and ambulatory wards, home nursing, and chronic disease care facilities. Study characteristics are summarized in Table 2 .

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Risk of bias was assessed as low among qualitative studies. Eleven studies were considered to have no concerns [ 23 , 25 - 32 , 34 , 35 ], while two had minor issues, including not reporting future research needs [ 22 ], and unclear statement of study’s aim [ 33 ]. One study had moderate concerns due to limited transparency in data saturation, data analysis, transferability, and reporting rigor [ 24 ]. Most qualitative studies clearly stated research aims, and all used appropriate methods, considered ethics, and reported findings.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Among cross-sectional studies, one was rated low risk, though it lacked a reported power analysis [ 37 ]. The other study had high risk of bias due to insufficient methodological detail, incomplete reporting of results, unclear data analysis rigor, and lack of generalizability [ 36 ]. Summaries of risk of bias assessments are presented in Figure 2 (qualitative) and Figure 3 (cross-sectional).

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Five interrelated themes were identified that describe how PA is enacted by HCPs to promote patient safety: interprofessional collaboration (12/16 studies), advocacy through risk prevention (11/16 studies), advocacy through education and informed consent (10/16 studies), safety-oriented advocacy (6/16 studies), and advocacy in medication management and polypharmacy (3/16 studies). Table 3 summarizes the key themes.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Interprofessional Collaboration in Patient Advocacy

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Twelve studies emphasized interprofessional collaboration as central to PA and safety [ 22 - 24 , 26 - 30 , 32 , 34 - 36 ]. HCPs described questioning constraints and navigating competing demands, engaging in strategic communication and expert-informed influence, and seeking allies or second opinions when feeling unheard, to guide collaborative clinical decision-making. HCPs acted as intermediaries between patients and colleagues, assessed patient conditions, and voiced safety concerns within multidisciplinary teams. HCPs also highlighted the use of clinical documentation systems, including targeted action-oriented notes, to promote alignment of care plans and continuity across disciplines, recognizing that individual actions and professional roles influenced overall team performance and patient outcomes.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Eleven studies described PA as enacted through proactive risk prevention strategies [ 23 , 27 - 30 , 32 - 37 ]. HCPs emphasized continuous health education, adherence to standards of care and reinforcing policies among colleagues, identification of treatment-related problems, and exploration of corrective solutions. Advocacy was expressed through the provision of empathic, evidence-based person-centered care, which supported early recognition of patient needs and potential safety risks. HCPs applied clinical judgment to prioritize safety, thoroughly assessed and documented diagnoses, tracked clinical procedures to anticipate challenges, and proactively developed post-discharge care plans. HCPs also reported maintaining situational awareness to proactively identify and address emerging clinical risks. They also recognized that supporting clinician mental and physical well-being is essential for maintaining composure, focus, and patient safety during high-pressure care delivery.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Patient Advocacy Through Education and Informed Consent

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Ten studies showed how HCPs advocated for safety through patient education and informed consent [ 23 - 26 , 28 - 31 , 35 , 36 ]. HCPs emphasized clear and inclusive communication with patients and all stakeholders regarding treatment plans, risks associated with treatment interruption or discontinuation, and potential medication interactions. HCPs highlighted exploring alternative administration methods, addressing patients’ fears and concerns, and ensuring patients understood available treatment options. They empowered patients to actively participate in care decisions by providing targeted education, and training for at-home care, including personalized simulations to support understanding and adherence.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Six studies described HCPs’ advocacy through safety-oriented clinical decision-making [ 23 , 26 , 31 , 33 , 35 , 37 ]. HCPs triaged patients to identify those requiring immediate attention, and prioritized safety-critical tasks to ensure accurate and safe care delivery. HCPs prioritized successful clinical outcomes, even when this involved trade-offs with patient comfort. HCPs also reported remaining attentive to patients’ pain and suffering, and prioritizing actions aimed at alleviating physical and emotional distress.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Three studies addressed advocacy in polypharmacy management [ 26 , 28 , 35 ]. Strategies included educating patients on proper medication administration and the risks of dose interruptions, conducting regular medication reviews, and coordinating with other HCPs to clarify and reconcile prescriptions. HCPs remained vigilant and shared responsibility in managing complex medication regimens among patients with multiple chronic conditions to prevent complications and ensure safe treatment.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Based on GRADE-CERQual assessment, the certainty of evidence ranged from low to moderate. Review findings with moderate confidence included polypharmacy and safety-oriented PA, while interprofessional collaboration, patient education and informed consent, and risk prevention were assessed as low confidence. A summary of the confidence assessment is provided in Table 4 .

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: This systematic review and thematic synthesis summarize how HCPs advocate for patient safety across diverse healthcare settings worldwide. Key PA themes included interprofessional collaboration, risk prevention, education and informed consent, safety, and medication management. The data presented describes HCPs’ advocacy practices when promoting patient safety in diverse healthcare settings worldwide.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Interprofessional collaboration was the most frequently identified theme, present in 12 of 16 included studies, suggesting it is a central mechanism through which HCPs enact PA to promote patient safety. This included working with multidisciplinary teams, raising concerns or disagreements about patients’ conditions and treatment plans, and maintaining thorough documentation to ensure continuous promotion of patient safety. Evidence from the literature indicates that HCPs in primary and secondary care settings agree that positive interprofessional relationships enhance PA and lead to improved patient outcomes [ 38 ].

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: For instance, Kucukarslan et al. (2003) reported that collaborating with pharmacists in intensive care settings reduced preventable adverse drug events (ADEs) by 78% (from 26.5 to 5.7 ADEs per 1000 patient-days), and shortened hospital stays by 1.4 days [ 39 ]. These findings highlight that effective collaboration enhances patient safety and improves efficiency of care. Collaboration and coordination among HCPs are essential for promoting integration across different levels of care. Strong communication channels not only strengthen these collaborative processes but also facilitate the transfer of important knowledge and continuity of care between secondary and primary care settings.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: By contrast, another qualitative study found that main barriers to establishing seamless care for patients included lack of trust among HCPs, a desire to control patient care in the hospital, lack of role clarity, and practical issues such as incompatibility of medical records systems [ 40 ]. Addressing these obstacles is essential for creating collaborative systems where PA can flourish.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Strategies aimed at reducing patient risks include ongoing professional education, guidelines compliance, vigilant monitoring, problem-solving, early discharge planning, and balancing patient autonomy with safety. Training has been found to be a critical enabler of patient safety, enhancing knowledge and perceived quality of care [ 41 ]. For example, implementation of the TeamSTEPPS program resulted in more than 50% improvement in patient safety culture scores from 98.42 ± 38.0 at baseline to 150.50 ± 10.7 post training and 151.1 ± 14.5 at two months, indicating sustained knowledge retention and positive cultural change [ 42 ]. Such programs show how structured, team-based training can strengthen advocacy and reduce harm.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Advocacy through education and informed consent emerged as an important contributor to patient safety. Respecting patients’ rights to receive clear information, participate in shared decision-making, and feel supported throughout their care journey is fundamental to high-quality clinical practice. This includes ensuring that patients understand treatment options, procedures, associated risks, and responsibilities related to post-discharge at-home care. Examples of advocacy-driven communication include obtaining informed consent before procedures, providing written post-discharge instructions, and implementing needs-based education [ 43 , 44 ]. Structured education has been shown to improve patient outcomes. For example, Ndosi et al. (2016) demonstrated that needs-based education significantly improved self-efficacy and symptom burden over time [ 45 ]. Such findings reinforce that patient education is an ethical obligation as well as an effective safety intervention.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Safety-oriented advocacy was reflected in how HCPs allocated limited resources toward the most urgent clinical needs, occasionally requiring trade-offs with patient comfort to achieve life-saving or clinically optimal outcomes. Ryynanen et al. (1998) reported that high-technology surgical interventions for life-threatening conditions and pain relief for terminally ill patients were consistently ranked as the highest priorities, while cosmetic procedures were considered lowest [ 46 ]. Similarly, Becker et al. (2015) found that 94.5% of patients presenting to the emergency room were discharged the same day, with 91.3% classified as low priority, while high-priority cases had significantly higher hospitalization rates, longer lengths of stay, and greater mortality [ 47 ]. These findings illustrate how safety-oriented advocacy relied on clinical prioritization to guide effective resource allocation, ensuring that patients with the most critical needs receive timely, safe, and effective care.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Advocacy in medication management and polypharmacy required vigilant monitoring, coordination, and patient engagement to ensure safe and effective treatment. Common strategies included conducting frequent medication reviews, clarifying prescriptions with colleagues, and educating patients on correct medication use. McIntosh et al. (2018) reported that patients aged ≥ 50 years taking five or more medications were most frequently identified for review, with structured medication discussions reducing the risk of adverse events [ 48 ]. Similarly, Gerard et al. (2020) found 500 (2.1 ± 3.9 per patient) discrepancies in 237 patients’ medical records, with 53.6% experiencing at least one inconsistency [ 49 ]. Pharmacists also identified 860 drug therapy problems (3.6 per patient), with 22.6% involving patients remaining untreated for a diagnosed indication [ 49 ]. Proactive advocacy-oriented medication management practices support error prevention, treatment adherence, and patient safety.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Overall, evidence suggests that PA practices contribute to improved patient safety and quality of care [ 12 ]. The results of this systematic review reinforce the importance of integrating PA into organizational culture with training, collaboration, and support structures that enable HCPs to voice concerns and act on behalf of patients. Addressing barriers such as time pressures, unclear roles, and system incompatibility remain essential [ 1 , 13 , 50 ]. Expanding advocacy training that integrates patient safety competencies may enhance HCPs’ capacity to translate advocacy skills into demonstrable improvements in clinical safety and quality of care.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: This review possesses several notable strengths. To our knowledge, it is the first thematic synthesis to systematically examine how HCPs enact PA practices to promote patient safety across multiple healthcare professions and international contexts. The inclusion of studies from 13 countries provides a global perspective and offers insights into cross-cultural and systemic variations in how advocacy is enacted in clinical practice. A systematic and transparent search and screening process, reported in accordance with PRISMA guidelines and informed by the PERSiST framework for qualitative synthesis, strengthen methodological credibility and replicability. Furthermore, the inclusion of qualitative studies offers a nuanced understanding of how practicing HCPs understand and enact PA in real-world clinical settings, adding contextual factors that may not be captured through quantitative measures alone. By integrating evidence from diverse settings and methodologies, this review provides a foundation for future research and the development of evidence-informed interventions that position advocacy as a mechanism for improving patient safety.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: This review also has several limitations. First, physicians were underrepresented in the included studies, limiting insight into their perspectives on PA and safety, and most participants were female. Most studies were qualitative, which provided rich understanding of complex interactions and informed potential practice and policy changes. However, these designs typically involved small samples and may be subject to research bias. The two cross-sectional studies included had methodological weaknesses: one lacked detail about the type of data collected, and neither reported power analyses to justify sample size, raising concerns about statistical validity.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Another limitation is the variability of study settings. Although the included studies spanned 13 countries, enhancing diversity, cultural and systemic differences restrict generalizability to other regions or healthcare systems. Additionally, only three databases were searched, and grey literature was excluded, so relevant unpublished or non-indexed studies may have been missed, and the possibility of publication bias cannot be ruled out. Furthermore, two records were identified through preliminary supplementary searching conducted prior to the formal database search. While both records were assessed using the same eligibility criteria and verified as peer-reviewed publications, full reproducibility of this identification step cannot be guaranteed given its preliminary nature.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: Future research should include a broader range of HCPs, particularly physicians and technicians, who play key roles in patient care but were largely absent from current studies. Quantitative and mixed-methods studies are needed to complement qualitative insights and to capture patient and caregiver perspectives on advocacy practices across different care settings and facility types. Research that includes multicultural and geographically diverse populations would help clarify how cultural context shapes advocacy behaviors. Finally, future work could examine the role of provider attributes such as emotional intelligence, moral intelligence, and PA engagement, which may influence how advocacy is enacted and perceived in clinical practice. Interventional studies, including training programs or organizational initiatives, could provide stronger evidence for strategies that promote effective PA and its effect on safety outcomes.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: This review synthesized evidence describing PA as a core mechanism for advancing patient safety and identified five key themes that explain how HCPs support patient safety across healthcare settings: interprofessional collaboration, risk prevention, education and informed consent, safety-oriented PA, and medication management and polypharmacy. Together, these themes highlight how HCPs promote safety through collaborative decision-making, proactive risk mitigation, patient engagement, clinical prioritization, and vigilant medication management, emphasizing the importance of communication, ethical decision-making, and clinical vigilance in minimizing risks and ensuring high-quality care.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.

text: The findings highlight the need for ongoing professional training to build advocacy-related skills, effective communication, and ethical decision-making. Education curricula should intentionally embed advocacy principles within core coursework and competency assessment, while healthcare organizations should align policies, training, and culture to support clinicians in acting as advocates for patient safety. Implementing these strategies may help reduce preventable harm, MEs, and SEs, ultimately advancing safer, more reliable, and patient-centered care delivery.

metadata.title: Healthcare Professional Advocacy Practices to Promote Patient Safety: A Systematic Review.
