metadata.title: Concerns and Experiences of Alcohol and Other Drug Healthcare Staff Regarding Intoxication Detection and Driving Safety of Opioid Treatment Clients

text: The data that support the findings of this study are available on request from the corresponding author. The data are not publicly available due to privacy or ethical restrictions.

metadata.title: Concerns and Experiences of Alcohol and Other Drug Healthcare Staff Regarding Intoxication Detection and Driving Safety of Opioid Treatment Clients

text: Acute lower respiratory tract infections (ALRIs) associated with respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) cause substantial morbidity and mortality in children under five years of age. An international pneumonia aetiology study found that RSV was the most frequent cause of severe pneumonia (31% of cases) [ 1 ]. Most RSV-related health burden (95% of morbidity and 97% of mortality) in children under five years old is observed in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs), and largely impacts infants aged under six months [ 2 ].

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: In Cameroon, ALRIs are a leading cause of childhood morbidity and mortality. In a hospital-based severe ALRI surveillance study conducted in Yaoundé, Cameroon, in 2011–2013, RSV was detected in 14% of children under five years of age [ 3 ]. In a similar setting during the COVID-19 pandemic, 22% of children seeking care for (severe) ALRI tested positive for RSV [ 4 ]. Surveillance data from 2018 to 2019 showed 13% average RSV-positivity per season across all age groups, with the highest burden in young children (74% of cases were children under five years of age) [ 5 ].

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: Recent years have seen surges in the development of pharmaceutical RSV preventive interventions [ 6 , 7 ]. Licensed RSV preventive interventions to protect infants present two strategies; first, infant immunisation with a single-dose of long-acting monoclonal antibody (mAb, further modelled as nirsevimab), administered before the RSV season or at birth [ 8 ]; second, a bivalent RSVpreF maternal vaccine administered once during the third trimester [ 9 , 10 ]. Both strategies allow for RSV prevention to be incorporated into well-established antenatal care and infant immunisation programmes, potentially strengthening them in the process. Further interventions are also emerging, including RSM01, a mAb in development by the Gates Medical Research Institute, specifically for use in LMICs [ 11 ].

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: The World Health Organization’s (WHO) Strategic Advisory Group of Experts on Immunisation (SAGE) assessed RSV preventive interventions and recommended them for global implementation [ 10 ]. National decision-makers will have to assess the value of a potential implementation in their setting. Facing the bulk of the RSV burden, LMICs stand to benefit most from RSV prevention. The health economic evidence needed to inform decision-making efforts is unavailable or scarce for many LMICs, including Cameroon. We aimed to provide an early assessment of the potential cost-effectiveness of RSV prevention in Cameroon, which can be updated as more data become available to guide data-driven decisions.

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: We modelled the potential health impact and cost-effectiveness of introducing the infant mAb and maternal vaccine for RSV prevention in children under five years of age in Cameroon over 10 years. We aggregated the lifetime costs and benefits associated with implementation of RSV interventions among 10 successive birth cohorts (2025–2034), as this allowed us to calculate the potential budget impact and explore changes in parameters over time. While RSV interventions are only expected to be impactful among infants, we included all children under five years of age for completeness. The primary outcome measure was the incremental cost per disability-adjusted life year (DALY) averted (in 2022 USD) from the government perspective, compared to the status quo (no pharmaceutical RSV preventive intervention). We performed iterative analyses for several scenarios described in detail below.

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: In the absence of a recognised national cost-effectiveness threshold (CET) in Cameroon, we calculated the probability that each intervention would be cost-effective over a range of thresholds expressed as proportions of the Cameroonian gross domestic product (GDP) per capita. We assumed a GDP per capita of USD 1563 informed by the World Bank estimate for 2022 [ 12 ]. Guided by convention, we used CETs of 0.5 and 1.0 GDP per capita, with an additional conservative CET of 0.1 GDP per capita. This conservative CET corresponded to the midpoint of the range estimated by Ochalek et al. for Cameroon (9–12% of GDP per capita) and was chosen because it accounts for opportunity costs of changes in health expenditure [ 13 ]. All costs are expressed in 2022 USD, and we used a discount rate of 3% per year for all future costs and benefits.

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: To calculate the potential impact and cost-effectiveness of both interventions, we used the universal vaccine decision-support model (UNIVAC), version 1.7 (London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine, London, UK), an Excel-based static proportionate outcomes model with a finely disaggregated age structure (week of age under five years) [ 14 ]. This open-access, user-friendly model promotes local ownership and balances model complexity and data paucity, often affecting LMICs. Its setup is described in detail elsewhere [ 15 ]. While the core methodology remains consistent with previous studies [ 15 – 18 ], we adjusted input parameters to reflect conditions in Cameroon.

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: Furthermore, we gathered secondary data from sources including the WHO and the United Nations Children’s Fund (UNICEF), the Demographic and Health Surveys (DHS, 2018, [ 19 ]), clinical trials and local experts ( Table 1 ). The study team convened with national stakeholders on 11 December 2023 to consult on and validate model input data and build consensus on the modelling strategy. Attending stakeholders were experts from the Expanded Programme on Immunisation Programme, Ministry of Public Health, international and national non-governmental organisations, academia, and national public health institutions in Cameroon. Subsequently, the UNIVAC model was populated to estimate the health and economic burden of RSV, and the impact and costs of introducing the infant mAb or maternal vaccine, expected over the studied period.

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: The UNIVAC model estimates the expected RSV health burden in the absence of preventive interventions (the status quo ) using population estimates from United Nations population projections [ 32 ] to calculate the total number of life-years lived before five years of age, and country-level rates of RSV outcomes. For Cameroon, previous RSV burden studies reported RSV outcomes for specific sub-populations with small sample sizes or were restricted to (urban) facility settings, thus exhibiting low generalizability [ 3 , 4 , 33 ]. Due to this restriction in national data, we used aggregate estimates of rates of RSV-related cases, hospital admissions, and deaths for countries in the relevant World Bank income classification ( i.e. lower-middle-income countries) estimated by Li et al. [ 2 ]. To estimate the rate of RSV-related outpatient visits, we assumed 59% access to pneumonia care as reported in the 2018 Cameroon DHS as a proxy for access to care for RSV ALRI [ 19 ]. We assigned RSV burden estimates to each week of age in children under five years using a Burr distribution fitted to UNIVAC default data from Argentina, Kenya, Mozambique, Pakistan, South Africa, and Vietnam, and studies with granular age data [ 2 , 15 ]. We assumed all RSV-related outcome events to be independent. This means that the same child can contribute to multiple outcome events. For example, a child with severe RSV admitted to the hospital following an outpatient visit would contribute to the rate of RSV cases, outpatient visits, and hospital admissions.

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: We accounted for costs associated with RSV-related outpatient visits and hospital admissions. For non-severe RSV, we estimated average costs for outpatient visits. For severe RSV, we estimated average costs associated with outpatient visits and hospital admissions, independently. Healthcare billing data in Cameroon were scarce, and the available national data for health services delivery costs lacked specificity. We estimated the facility-based healthcare costs associated with RSV infection, using 2021 WHO Choosing Interventions that are Cost-Effective (CHOICE) [ 22 ] estimates of health services delivery costs, and estimates of diagnosis and treatment costs provided by national stakeholders based on experience with bronchiolitis and pneumonia care. Since our study explored a government perspective, we omitted all out-of-pocket costs. The diagnosis and treatment costs were adjusted to exclude the average out-of-pocket proportion of healthcare expenditure (68%) in Cameroon [ 34 ]. RSV-specific cost of illness data from Malawi and South Africa informed the uncertainty ranges [ 23 , 24 ].

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: Since neither of these interventions have been introduced in comparable settings, we used proxy data to inform expected coverage for RSV birth-dose and maternal vaccination programmes. For the infant mAb, we assumed birth-dose scheduling for Bacillus Calmette-Guérin (BCG) vaccine as a proxy. We informed BCG coverage by WHO/UNICEF estimates of national immunisation coverage (69% in 2022) [ 25 ]. BCG coverage from the 2017 cohort (87%, DHS 2018 [ 19 ]) informed the upper bound of the uncertainty range to represent a higher realistic coverage previously achieved for the BCG vaccine in Cameroon [ 19 ]. Similarly, we assumed the real-world timeliness (coverage by week of age) estimated for BCG would be a reasonable proxy for the real-world timeliness of an RSV infant mAb. Therefore, we assumed that doses with delayed administration have an impact on RSV disease later in infancy.

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: Although 71% of recent births in Cameroon were protected against neonatal tetanus [ 19 ], maternal tetanus vaccination was a challenging proxy for RSV maternal vaccination due to irregular vaccination schedules depending on parity and tetanus immunisation status preceding pregnancy, as well as obscure timeliness in practice [ 35 ]. Instead, we considered attendance at antenatal care (ANC) visits to be an appropriate proxy for achievable RSV maternal vaccine coverage because it represents the potential for access to a skilled healthcare provider within the target gestation window for RSV vaccination (24–36 or 32–36 weeks). We assumed the maternal vaccine could achieve coverage equal to the proportion of pregnant women attending at least four ANC visits (64.9%). In Cameroon, only 41% of first ANC visits in 2018 were during the first trimester [ 19 ], thus reducing the chances of attending four ANC visits. To address this potential underestimation, we used the proportion of women attending at least one ANC visit (87%) as the upper bound of the uncertainty range. We also used a more conservative lower bound (55%) based on estimates by Baral et al. of maternal vaccine coverage within 24–36 weeks of gestation, accounting for ANC timing [ 27 ].

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: Based on evidence from phase 3 clinical trials in term and (late) pre-term infants, we used two efficacy estimates for each intervention corresponding to two trial endpoints: RSV-related lower respiratory tract infection (LRTI) hospital admissions, representing severe RSV outcomes, and any medically attended RSV-related LRTI representing non-severe RSV outcomes. We included efficacy against non-severe outcomes as a proportion of efficacy against severe RSV. For the infant mAb, the two efficacy estimates were very close; however, the trial reported a higher lower bound value for efficacy against non-severe RSV (95% confidence interval (CI) = 62–85) than severe RSV (95% CI = 49–89) [ 26 ], resulting in a proportion >1 (0.62/0.49). Due to technical limitations, we omitted the CI and used the central-value proportion. We assumed efficacy to be constant over time and fall to zero after the trial observational period, which defined the duration of protection, with an uncertainty range of ±1 month.

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: We estimated the net costs of each intervention as the difference between the total programme costs of the intervention and the averted healthcare costs resulting from immunisation. The programme costs included all costs of providing the intervention from procuring to administering the infant mAb or maternal vaccine. Because the pricing for both interventions is highly uncertain, we assumed equal prices for them (USD 5 per dose for the base-case analysis). RSV interventions were included as part of Gavi, the Vaccine Alliance’s Vaccine Investment Strategy 2018 for the period 2021–2025 [ 36 ]. While Cameroon was eligible for Gavi support, at the time of writing, there was no clarity on the modalities and level of support that may be offered by Gavi to eligible countries. We decided to exclude any Gavi support from our analysis to reflect the full potential costs of introducing the infant mAb or maternal vaccine.

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: We applied intervention coverage and efficacy assumptions to estimate age-specific RSV burden with and without each of the RSV preventive interventions. We calculated the lifetime health effects for all RSV disease outcomes (cases, outpatient visits, hospital admissions, and deaths), culminating in DALYs averted, and expressed as their proportional decrease once the intervention parameters are applied (percentage reduction). Similarly, we calculated the economic impact of RSV preventive interventions as the difference in RSV-related healthcare costs for outpatient visits and hospital admissions with and without intervention, from the governmental perspective (healthcare costs averted). In Cameroon, RSV activity has previously been described to persist most of the year with peaks from September to February, and April to July [ 3 , 4 ]. For simplicity, we assumed steady RSV incidence throughout the year and year-round immunisation.

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: To account for uncertainty in input parameters, we ran deterministic and probabilistic scenario analyses. We performed deterministic scenario analyses adjusting the intervention price and efficacy assumptions:

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: − Intervention price per dose increased to USD 10 and USD 15.

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: − Waning efficacy: as described by Mahmud et al. [ 15 ], we assumed a gamma-fitted efficacy scenario, whereby efficacy declines gradually, approaching zero at 12 months.

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: − Exploratory analyses: we estimated the price threshold under which both interventions may be considered cost-effective.

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: While deterministic analyses used the central values of input parameters, probabilistic sensitivity analyses utilised the uncertainty ranges. We assumed simple PERT-Beta distributions informed by the uncertainty ranges and ran 1000 Monte-Carlo simulations for each intervention at a fixed price per dose (USD 5, USD 10, and USD 15). In each simulation, the model probabilistically selected input values contained within the uncertainty ranges and calculated the incremental cost per DALY averted. We present the results from these simulations using cost-effectiveness acceptability curves, showing the probability that each intervention would be cost-effective at a CET equal to 0.1, 0.5, and 1.0 GDP per capita (indicated by the point where a chosen CET intersects the curves). These illustrations provide a visual reference for policy makers when evaluating the potential introduction of RSV preventive strategies in Cameroon under uncertainty.

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: Without intervention, we estimated RSV to contribute 204 554 DALYs, including 8291 deaths, in the 10-year cohort of children aged under five years ( Table 2 ). In our base-case scenario, both the infant mAb (USD 5 per dose, 69% coverage, 77% efficacy, five months protection) and maternal vaccine (USD 5 per dose, 65% coverage, 69% efficacy, six months protection) exhibited potential for a substantial impact in Cameroon. We estimated the infant mAb to avert 56502 DALYs and 2267 deaths (corresponding to a 27.3% reduction in RSV mortality among children under five years old). Over the modelled period, RSV prevention using infant mAb cost USD 49 million and averted USD 21 million in RSV-associated healthcare costs (net cost = USD 28 million). We estimated that the maternal vaccine averted 55 420 DALYs and 2226 deaths (corresponding to a 26.9% reduction in RSV mortality among children under five years old). With slightly lower programme costs (USD 47 million) and impact on RSV-associated healthcare costs (USD 19 million) than the infant mAb, the maternal vaccine had a similar estimated net cost (USD 28 million).

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: Given base-case assumptions, the infant mAb and maternal vaccine were equally cost-effective (USD 500 per DALY averted) when each was compared to the status quo (no preventive intervention) ( Figure 1 ). This incremental cost corresponded to 32% of the Cameroonian GDP per capita , thus considered cost-effective at the conventional CETs of 0.5 and 1.0 GDP per capita , but overstepping the conservative 0.1 GDP per capita CET. Given base-case assumptions, the infant mAb and maternal vaccine must be priced no higher than USD 2.36 and 2.30 per dose, respectively, to be cost-effective at this conservative CET. The estimated incremental costs reported above were highly sensitive to input parameters. Since the two strategies yielded substantially similar incremental costs, subtle changes in input parameters easily changed the rank order of the two strategies. Therefore, to avoid potentially misleading estimates, we did not model head-to-head comparisons; instead, each RSV immunisation strategy was compared with the status quo .

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: In the price scenarios, the incremental costs per DALY averted were USD 1137 at USD 10 per dose and USD 1773 at USD 15 per dose of the infant mAb; and USD 1122 at USD 10 per dose and USD 1743 at USD 15 per dose of the maternal vaccine. The infant mAb was slightly more costly (less cost-effective) than the maternal vaccine at higher prices per dose (Table S1 in the Online Supplementary Document ). Incremental costs for both interventions exceeded the 0.1 GPD per capita CET at all assumed prices. However, at prices per dose of USD 5 and USD 10, both interventions were cost-effective at the 0.5 and 1.0 GDP per capita CETs, respectively. At USD 15 per dose, the incremental costs for both interventions exceeded the 1.0 GDP per capita CET.

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: When we adjusted the base-case scenario to assume waning efficacy, both interventions yielded higher impact, averting 69 384 (infant mAb) and 62 032 (maternal vaccine) DALYs among children under five years old over 10 years (Table S2 in the Online Supplementary Document ). The incremental cost was USD 336 per DALY averted for the infant mAb and USD 412 per DALY averted for the maternal vaccine. In this scenario, compared to the status quo , the infant mAb was more cost-effective than the maternal vaccine, and both interventions were cost-effective at the 0.5 GDP per capita CET ( Figure 1 ).

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: In probabilistic sensitivity analyses, 1000 Monte Carlo simulations estimated varying incremental costs and benefits of the infant mAb and maternal vaccine, compared to the status quo , forming overlapping estimate clouds across price scenarios (Figures S1 and S2 in the Online Supplementary Document ). The resulting cost-effectiveness acceptability curves suggested that at the 0.5 GDP per capita CET (USD 782), the infant mAb and maternal vaccine were >90% likely to be cost-effective if priced USD≤5 per dose. However, cost-effectiveness was unlikely at the conservative 0.1 GDP per capita CET, even at USD 5 per dose (probability <20%) for both interventions ( Figure 2 ). Appropriate product pricing, with potential Gavi support, could make one or both interventions cost-effective at this conservative threshold.

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: Here, we provide an early assessment of the potential impact and cost-effectiveness of introducing the RSV infant mAb or maternal vaccine in Cameroon. The presented analyses utilised the most reliable data available for Cameroon, and assumptions were validated by a group of experts. Given our assumptions, both interventions showed potential for substantial impact in Cameroon, possibly averting more than 55 000 DALYs and over a quarter of the RSV-related deaths expected over 10 years among children under five years old. As prices for both interventions remain uncertain and strongly affect cost-effectiveness, we provide independent acceptability estimates for both interventions at several prices and CETs. We assessed incremental costs of each intervention vs. the status quo at each price per dose, allowing for parallel evaluation of cost-effectiveness. Although we assumed equal prices per dose, it is likely that the infant mAb and maternal vaccine will become available at different costs. Reasons for this include a pricing agreement in place for LMICs for Pfizer’s RSV maternal vaccine and the widely held belief that the mAb cost of goods will be significantly higher than for a maternal vaccine [ 37 ].

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: Additionally, we identified the break-even price per dose at which both interventions are cost-effective at selected CETs. To be cost-effective at the most conservative CET (0.1 GDP per capita ) [ 13 ], and using our parameter assumptions, the infant mAb and maternal vaccine must be priced below USD 2.50 per dose. While break-even prices may aid decision makers in assessing affordability, they should be applied with caution since they are based on model parameters we assumed in deterministic analyses and sensitive to changes in input data.

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: Cost-effectiveness of the two interventions differed only marginally within pricing scenarios, with the variation most likely driven by subtle differences in assumed efficacy or duration of protection. Cost-effectiveness improved when we assumed the intervention efficacy to be instantaneously high and decline over time, lending preference to the infant mAb over the maternal vaccine (USD 336 vs. USD 412 per DALY averted) due to a higher cumulative efficacy. Our analysis remains conservative, as it disregards any strategies to improve affordability beyond product pricing, including Gavi support, to reflect the full price of introducing either intervention. The modalities and level of Gavi support may heavily influence cost-effectiveness, and this analysis should be updated as they become clearer.

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: The cost-effectiveness of both interventions differs only slightly, and a cost-driven preference depends on the currently unknown price per dose. Other factors not accounted for in this analysis could help more precisely determine the dominant intervention. These include public perception and acceptance of maternal and newborn immunisation, and the level of available infrastructure for implementation, including trained providers, sufficient cold chain logistics, and access to postnatal care and ANC. Country readiness assessments are needed to address these factors. Furthermore, the majority of RSV-related DALYs were attributable to mortality. One might consider an impact-driven approach in the implementation of RSV preventive interventions. For example, targeted prevention efforts in communities where access to care is limited, and thus chances of survival are decreased, will likely considerably enhance our estimated health impact.

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: Our study has several limitations, mostly resulting from data paucity. We maximised the reliability of proxy data by comparing available sources and selecting data that were most recent, complete, and most applicable to the Cameroonian context, and accounted for uncertainties in probabilistic analyses. We recognise the potential for inaccuracies introduced by using proxy data and recommend follow-up assessments as more data become available. We assumed year-round, unrestricted administration of interventions. Recommendations for seasonal or population-specific administration are likely to change the results presented here, though seasonal approaches require rapid adaptability and may be challenging in low-resource settings. Notably, there are few descriptions of RSV seasonal patterns in Cameroon, and available studies were restricted to short periods and single regions. This gap poses a challenge to a seasonal implementation strategy. We did not factor in discrepancies in equitable access to RSV immunisation. In Cameroon, the populations of zero-dose children and women receiving no ANC remain substantial, with considerable sub-regional differences [ 19 ]. Targeted immunisation programmes to reach them may yield higher impact while simultaneously requiring higher programmatic investments. Both infant immunisation and ANC services may stand to benefit from the introduction of RSV immunisation.

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: Despite international trial recruitment, the efficacy estimates that we used in this study came from trials based mostly in high-income-countries, with lower severe RSV outcome rates compared to LMICs such as Cameroon. It is noteworthy that participants of a controlled clinical trial differ significantly from average LMIC populations. Because pregnant populations in Cameroon have higher rates of complications, including HIV/AIDS, than do trial participants, antibody levels at birth following maternal vaccination may not achieve the reported trial efficacy. Similarly, due to underpowered groups, it is uncertain what level of protection maternal vaccination offers preterm infants. Although mathematical models provide some insights [ 38 ], scenarios favouring maternal vaccines may benefit from catch-up programmes with the infant mAb for unprotected high-risk infants [ 11 ]. A study in sub-Saharan African countries is evaluating RSV maternal vaccination efficacy and safety in response to a numerical increase in preterm births observed in two upper-middle-income country maternal vaccine trial sites [ 10 ]. Our study excluded model inputs on intervention safety in the absence of conclusive data. Understanding the impact of RSV prevention for populations in comparable settings will provide more insight into the potential impact expected in Cameroon.

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: Lastly, we excluded the impact of indirect effects of RSV prevention, such as changes in healthcare capacity, and opportunity costs of healthcare expenditure as well as excluded RSV-related societal costs, including out-of-pocket healthcare expenditure. This is due to a lack of RSV-specific healthcare utilisation and cost data in Cameroon. We used WHO-CHOICE cost data and expert estimates of costs for pneumonia care as base-case proxies. We also used RSV-specific costing data from the healthcare system perspective in Malawi and South Africa. These estimates varied considerably from the base-case assumption, as well as from each other, and thus informed the uncertainty range. Modelling RSV-related costs from the governmental perspective only underestimated the overall economic impact of RSV prevention. Therefore, ascertaining a measure of household costs for RSV-related care in Cameroon would enable a valuable expansion of the current work.

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: We provide the first assessment of the potential impact and cost-effectiveness of RSV preventive interventions in Cameroon – the infant mAb (nirsevimab) and maternal vaccine (bivalent RSVpreF). Using the UNIVAC decision-support model, our analysis suggests that both interventions could substantially reduce RSV-related morbidity and mortality among children under five years old, potentially preventing over 2000 deaths among children under-five years of age over 10 years, with comparable cost-effectiveness profiles. While these estimates are limited by the scarcity of country-specific data, they provide the best available approximation for the Cameroonian context and address a crucial health economic data gap at a time when large-scale RSV prevention becomes available. Future analyses should update these findings as more (country-specific) data become available, particularly describing RSV-related burden, healthcare utilisation and costs, and product pricing. The use of the UNIVAC decision-support model [ 14 ] enables local stakeholders to readily incorporate new evidence and make data-driven decisions on RSV prevention in Cameroon. Decision-makers may evaluate both interventions and assess programmatic feasibility to establish the best-suited strategy for RSV prevention in Cameroon. Apart from factors such as existing infrastructure and public perception, the product price needs to be established. To ensure accessibility to RSV immunisation, these interventions must be priced reasonably. Efforts to accommodate affordability may include tiered pricing, multi-dose vial presentations [ 11 ], or licensing of generic products. Gavi support is likely to improve the affordability of RSV preventive interventions.

metadata.title: Impact and cost-effectiveness of two interventions to prevent paediatric respiratory syncytial virus disease in Cameroon: a modelling approach.

text: Cervical cancer accounts for over 300,000 deaths annually, with 90% of these deaths occurring in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs) [ 1 ]. Many women will die from cervical cancer not because of ineffective prevention and treatment but due to unequal access to the highly cost-effective human papillomavirus (HPV) vaccination and cervical cancer screening [ 2 ]. Governments and international organizations have thus invested heavily in increasing vaccination to address cervical cancer mortality, [ 3 , 4 ] as the HPV vaccine is the most cost-effective solution to cervical cancer prevention [ 5 ]. Initiatives such as the Gavi, the Vaccine Alliance (originally Global Alliance for Vaccines and Immunization), [ 6 ] the Gardasil Access Program, [ 7 , 8 ] and the direct investment by many governments in vaccine acquisition have provided millions of doses globally. As of 2025, 148 countries have integrated the HPV vaccination into their routine immunization programs, including 80 of the 136 countries classified as LMICs by the World Bank [ 9 ].

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: Even in countries with national vaccine coverage, vaccination rates vary widely from 91% in Sweden to 74% in the UK to 31% in Kenya.4 We conceptualize a framework that broadly characterizes the reasons for variable uptake into issues of either (1) access or (2) education. Access-related barriers include the costs of the vaccine and limitations in the human and physical infrastructure to deliver the vaccine [ 10 ]. Educational challenges encompass lack of awareness and knowledge, fear, political pushback, and stigma [ 11 ].

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: The most effective way to increase vaccination is to provide access to the vaccine. A plethora of implementation techniques used in LMICs to introduce the vaccine have led to vaccine uptake of at least 60%, suggesting that there are many effective approaches. However, because of the lack of formal implementation science in the LMIC context, policymakers looking to create future vaccine campaigns have limited data to identify the most effective HPV vaccine implementation strategy. Here we review these data and provide a list of best practices in creating vaccine campaigns. In addition to vaccine implementation, we review the evidence from LMICs on providing effective education on the HPV vaccine. The HPV vaccine poses unique challenges compared to other vaccines, including its perceived association with sexual promiscuity and the age of the target population (preteens and adolescents). These unique challenges necessitate tailored educational approaches that focus on educating both adolescents, parents, and the wider community.

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: To generate this narrative review, we performed a hand review of literature indexed on PubMed and Google Scholar to collect a corpus of articles. We also used relevant search terms to supplement the hand literature review (Supplementary material 1: Search terms). Additionally, we reviewed publicly available government documents as many of the vaccine campaigns are only published on non-peer-reviewed government websites and memoranda. We focus on data from countries classified as an LMIC by the World Bank at the time the study was conducted and used the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation (GRADE) system to stratify studies by their quality and focus on the highest quality data [ 12 ]. We draw primarily from the HPV literature but also include relevant information from other vaccines and health campaigns in LMICs to outline effective strategies for introducing and educating about the HPV vaccine. We have compiled a list of best practices for developing effective vaccination and educational campaigns in the hope that this information is useful to policymakers, healthcare providers, and educators looking to optimize future HPV vaccination campaigns and design research studies in LMICs.

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: HPV vaccine campaigns tend to have the highest uptake when the vaccine is brought to the child, rather than having it available at a centralized location, such as a local health center. Typically, this means doing the vaccine campaign at schools. Due to the target age for immunization (9–14 years), schools represent a convenient location for vaccine delivery that does not introduce additional travel barriers [ 7 , 13 ]. Schools are often community hubs and can serve as a natural place for vaccine education and sensitization information to be posted for children and their parents [ 14 ], while also being cost-effective at the implementation level by using existing physical infrastructure [ 15 ]. Table 1 lists the key studies that inform the best practices we recommend in Table 2 .

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: School-based approaches have been used successfully in a number of national HPV vaccine campaigns including in Nigeria, South Africa, and Rwanda which achieved vaccine uptake rates of 71%, 87%, and 93%, respectively [ 16 , 40 , 46 ]. There are no randomized trials of implementation strategies, but there is a broad range of evidence supporting school-based HPV vaccinations across numerous LMICs in various global regions. LaMontagne et al.’s cross-sectional survey looked at HPV campaigns and their endline vaccine rates in Peru, Uganda, Vietnam, and India, finding that school-based vaccination campaigns all reached at least 83% of the target population in these countries [ 17 ]. Riviere et al. directly compared HPV vaccine uptake in school-based and non-school-based educational groups in Haiti, with the school group achieving 92% vaccine uptake compared to 77% in the non-school group [ 18 ]. Multiple systematic reviews further support the conclusion that school-based immunization is effective across many different LMICs [ 7 , 8 , 41 , 47 , 48 ]. The efficacy of school-based HPV vaccination has also been demonstrated in high-income countries (HICs) such as Australia, Canada, and the UK [ 47 ]. These data form the basis of our recommendation to prioritize school-based HPV vaccination for adolescents.

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: Effective campaigns aimed for broad vaccination and involved pushes to educate and sensitize children, their parents, and the broader community. A large campaign has the added benefit of excitement and often high levels of investment from stakeholders, leadership, and collaborators [ 7 , 16 , 19 ] as compared to smaller sustained efforts that do not seek mass vaccination in a short period of time. Now that data suggest a single dose of the HPV vaccine may be sufficient [ 49 ], large campaigns designed to deliver as many vaccines as is feasible directly to children (i.e., at school) are likely to be highly efficacious in reducing cervical cancer risk at a population level. Because of the single dose, designing multi-time point interventions may no longer be necessary for population-level prevention of cervical cancer. Additionally, if single-dose vaccinations are used, media campaigns and events can be used to increase vaccination targeted toward a single day for larger populations. Most of the interventions we review use a multi-dose schedule for HPV vaccination, but single-dose campaigns will be more cost-effective, assuming long-term data continue to show that a single dose is sufficient. While we recommend campaigns because they have been successful in several LMICs, there are data to suggest that adding the vaccine to the routine schedule can also be effective, especially if there are concerns about the costs of a campaign, though rapid uptake by the population is likely more limited without broad awareness of vaccine availability [ 48 ].

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: Though school-based immunization is a common and effective strategy for HPV vaccination in LMICs, alternative locations and methods are needed to reach high-risk populations and out-of-school children. Reaching out-of-school children is an important challenge for many LMICs. In the Democratic Republic of Congo, Suriname, and Equatorial Guinea, the majority of girls in the target age for HPV vaccination are not in school [ 50 ], and an estimated 18.6 million girls in Sub-Saharan Africa are not enrolled in primary school as of 2018 [ 51 ]. Furthermore, non-school-based vaccination strategies are valuable for high-risk adult populations, including young adults and sex workers [ 20 , 21 ]. Vaccinating high-risk and hard-to-reach populations has less impact on population health compared to mass vaccination of all children, but reaching these demographics is important to broadly maximize immunization, reduce individual risk, and maintain health equity.

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: Vaccination at health centers can lead to high vaccine uptake, occasionally with numerically similar results to school-based immunization. Some studies even report higher vaccination rates at health centers than at schools among adolescents. For example, LaMontagne et al. reported uptake rates of 94% and 98% at health centers compared to 83% and 96% at schools in the same years in Vietnam using randomized samples of girls from eligible households [ 17 ].

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: However, Ladner et al.’s analysis of Gardasil Access Programs found that mixed or school-based campaigns achieved higher percent uptake on average (> 74% versus > 70% for health centers) and that school-based interventions often reach more children [ 7 ]. Luciani et al.’s review of Latin American HPV vaccination strategies similarly found that school-based or mixed school and health center immunizations consistently achieved rates > 80% in at least one age group in every country studied compared to rates as low as 30% for health center-based vaccination [ 41 ]. Additionally, Bhutan’s national HPV vaccination program experienced a 15–20% drop in vaccine uptake in years using health center vaccinations compared to years using a school-based strategy [ 52 ].

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: The data thus suggest that HPV immunization strategies based solely at health centers have varied uptake and tend to be less effective than school-based strategies. Because of this—as well as the selection bias, need for travel, and need to add infrastructure for education associated with health center strategies—we do not recommend exclusively conducting vaccination through health centers. Rather, we suggest that health centers can be an effective hub for reaching out-of-school adolescents. For example, one campaign in Brazil provided an alternative date/place for vaccination for girls who were not absent from or not in school at the time of in-school vaccination. This reminder program used phone calls and provided the vaccine free of charge at a nearby health center [ 22 ]. This strategy led to first-dose vaccination of an additional 279 girls, which represented 20% of the total who received the first dose of the vaccine. Rwanda’s national vaccination program similarly targeted out-of-school and absent-from-school girls, using community health workers to trace unvaccinated girls and then provided the vaccine at a local health center [ 16 ]. Through this labor-intensive mobilization strategy, Rwanda was able to vaccinate an estimated 46% of the out-of-school target population of 4681 adolescent girls.

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: Targeted interventions for specific adult populations such as sex workers have also achieved high vaccination rates when conducted at health centers [ 21 ], but are often separate from those aimed at children due to fundamental differences in each population. A health center-based approach could therefore provide coverage to key populations but likely will not achieve the broad population-level impact of mass vaccination of adolescents. A major need for health center-based vaccinations is awareness-building among the target population, for which sensitization and educational materials and community health workers are valuable resources, as demonstrated by Rwanda’s approach with community health workers [ 16 ].

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: Another studied approach that has been used to reach out-of-school girls is a mother-daughter approach. This typically couples the HPV vaccine for the child with cervical cancer screening for the mother using community health workers. This strategy increased HPV vaccination by 11% and achieved 85% uptake of cervical cancer screening in a community in Mali [ 23 ] and led to 6851 girls in Cameroon receiving a single dose of the vaccine [ 24 ]. However, we have concerns about the ability to reach children who are not coupled with their mother, which should be considered with this implementation approach especially in refugee and displaced populations.

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: Ultimately, when considering vaccination strategies outside of schools, it is important to consider cultural context to determine the best way to achieve high vaccine uptake rates. The central principle we recommend is to remove barriers to vaccine access through delivery to the individual, with local adaptation of strategies between and within countries to maximize vaccine uptake.

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: Context-specific strategies from other vaccine delivery strategies could be adapted for the HPV vaccine, especially for populations that are not reached with an initial broad wave. These more resource-intensive approaches are effective, but when simply providing the vaccine and investing in a campaign can achieve vaccine rates of > 70%, these are best reserved for harder-to-reach populations after broad initial population coverage. Examples from other vaccine strategies include small cash incentives, which were shown to increase the use of the Bacillus Calmette-Guérin (BCG) tuberculosis vaccination in infants when offered to parents in Pakistan [ 53 ]. In Sierra Leone, mobile clinics increased the COVID-19 vaccination rate in remote areas of the country by 26% [ 54 ]. Other intervention strategies show ingenuity in creating culture-specific solutions. For example, a study conducted in Nigeria utilized the social capital of older women to educate mothers in urban slums about infant vaccine schedule adherence [ 55 ]. These and other intervention strategies are worth considering when designing HPV vaccine interventions, particularly to reach out-of-school adolescents and in catch-up campaigns.

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: Making the HPV vaccine available is the most important factor for vaccinating a population. However, education and sensitization are critical to inform the community and maintain trust with healthcare providers. Misinformation and rumors have a lasting impact on community health. For example, the Japanese government suspended national recommendation for the HPV vaccine between 2013 and 2022 due to sensationalized media backlash, resulting in a generation of women with higher rates of abnormal cervical cytology [ 56 ]. Similar cases of public backlash can be reduced (though not eliminated) through effective sensitization and education.

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: Though education is broadly accepted as important for improving HPV vaccine awareness and uptake, there is limited evidence on how best to implement the education, and few studies grounded in implementation science frameworks. In general, most strategies grounded in learning theory appear to be effective, though with poor quality evidence. Table 1 includes interventional HPV vaccine education studies from LMICs. The most studied education approach is a traditional lecture or discussion-based education by an expert from outside the community. As can be seen in the GRADE evaluations, these studies are generally of very poor or poor design. In general, children in school learn when taught new information and can demonstrate short-term knowledge gains. Thus, without unique components of the interventions, coupling to vaccine receipt data, or long-term follow-up, many of these studies do not add substantially to the literature. We list methodological limitations that we identified and solutions for future researchers in Supplementary material 1: Table S2. Additional research and comparative studies are needed to solidify the evidence base for HPV vaccine education in LMICs.

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: Due to the relatively older target age of the HPV vaccine (preteens and young teenagers), a common sensitization strategy involves educating these children themselves, rather than just their parents. The highest quality educational study by Zhang et al. featured thoughtful randomization and long-term follow-up of knowledge retention [ 45 ]. Researchers reported the impact of a schoolteacher-led intervention in China, in which teachers of students aged 13–14 were trained to lead lectures and discussions about HPV risk and prevention ( n = 5024) [ 45 ]. Students in both the control and intervention groups completed a questionnaire before, immediately after, and 1 year following the intervention, which was a 45-min lecture about HPV and the HPV vaccine presented by their teacher. Students in the intervention group showed significant improvements in vaccine knowledge and acceptability both immediately after the intervention and 1 year later. This study leveraged an effective educational strategy with a teach-the-teacher approach, leveraging the local teacher’s relationships and knowledge on how to effectively educate their classes rather than have an external expert provide the education.

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: While most educational strategies we reviewed resulted in immediate increases in knowledge and vaccine acceptance, there remains a large gap in evidence connecting these educational interventions to either (a) long-term knowledge retention and/or (b) increased vaccination status. Increased short-term knowledge does not necessarily correlate with increased vaccination: in Escoffery et al.’s meta-analysis, there were 47 studies that measured HPV vaccination after education, and only 9 found significantly increased vaccine uptake [ 43 ]. The question for HPV vaccination education aimed toward children and adolescents ultimately is not “Can children learn?” but rather “What educational approach improves immunization rates?” There remains a need for foundational evidence supporting what is the optimal educational intervention for the HPV vaccine. Today, the data in Zhang et al. remains the highest quality evidence for building HPV educational interventions aimed at adolescents [ 45 ].

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: In Supplementary material 1: Table S3, we highlight a few studies that use novel educational interventions largely as inspiration for designing unique interventions. Some of these learning formats—such as peer education and educational comic books—have been studied in the context of HPV vaccination in communities in the USA [ 57 – 59 ], but they have the same major methodologic flaws with limited data on vaccine outcomes and long-term knowledge retention. These remain exciting areas for active study especially when grounded in learning theory and implementation science.

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: Educating adolescents is key to increasing vaccination, but parental consent is often requisite for vaccine delivery [ 47 ]. In our own work in Nigeria, children who were eligible for the vaccine but did not receive it were most likely to cite a lack of parental consent as the reason for non-vaccination [ 60 ]. While there are many reasons parents may refuse the vaccine for their children, a lack of knowledge is a major reason for vaccine hesitancy. Parent and community education is thus important for ensuring vaccine uptake among adolescents.

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: Community-based education tends to be effective for engaging and educating parents and community members. The use of community health workers in particular has improved HPV vaccine awareness and uptake in multiple LMICs [ 23 , 25 , 26 , 61 ], through strategies such as door-to-door outreach and guided discussions, achieving vaccination rates as high as 95%. Community health workers understand their community and come with increased trust, compared to external persons from government or vaccine groups. It is important to note that, as is the case with adolescent education, there are few published educational interventions with data supporting long-term knowledge retention in parents—only one adult education study we found had follow-up beyond 3 months [ 21 ]. However, the use of community health workers and community-based research has a broad evidence base outside of the HPV vaccine supporting its use [ 44 , 62 – 64 ]. Thus, using a community-based approach is an excellent starting place, with local adaptation of the approach.

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: Another strategy for reaching parents is short message service (SMS) messaging, which has been studied for HPV vaccination reminders in Uganda [ 27 ] and Nigeria [ 28 ], but lack a broad evidence base. Reminder systems are commonly used as a low-cost way to increase uptake for many vaccines in LMICs, though without the social capital of a face-to-face interaction their net impact may be limited relative to the effect of community health workers [ 65 ]. Because there is more limited evidence on the effect of automated messages on education and vaccine uptake, we recommend community-based approaches as the primary education strategy. Reminder systems are low cost, likely low risk in many populations, and are reasonable to be used as an adjunct strategy. We find that community education campaigns and other types of sensitization strategies work well when embedded within larger vaccine campaigns rather than being temporally separated from vaccine availability. This allows any strategy that has a positive short-term impact to potentially translate into increased vaccination. Especially within the context of a single-dose campaign, this can be powerful in increasing the vaccination rate even if the education has limited long-term impact on knowledge.

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: We believe this narrative review represents an overview of available evidence but has some limitations due to its narrative format. That this is a narrative review without rigorous search methodology is a limitation of this article; a systematic review may find additional evidence that was not included here due to hand review limitations. Additionally, our inclusion criteria were limited to studies conducted in countries classified as an LMIC by the World Bank in 2025. Vaccination and education strategies conducted in HICs that are applicable to LMIC settings may have been missed.

metadata.title: Improving human papillomavirus vaccine uptake in low- and middle-income countries: a narrative review of implementation and education strategies.

text: HPV vaccination is an important and cost-effective strategy for decreasing future cancer burden for cervical and other cancer types, especially in LMICs. Available literature suggests high vaccination rates can be achieved by simply providing access to the vaccine in a convenient location and ensuring that communities are aware it is available. We advocate for school-based vaccination approaches based on the evidence available, with additional campaign arms designed to reach out-of-school children. Education is foundational for increasing awareness and dispelling misinformation, but there is limited high-quality evidence on the optimal ways to educate children and adults on the HPV vaccine. It is therefore difficult to recommend specific education strategies other than those that are grounded in learning theory and effective in other contexts. Examples include train-the-trainer approaches [ 66 ] and the use of community health workers [ 67 ]. Future research should focus on creating a robust evidence base for HPV vaccine implementation and education, particularly concerning one-dose HPV vaccination strategies that will improve global population health.
